constraints: Keep the protagonist mostly silent
Use only a few concise lines at important moments
Let recurring characters and factions carry most dialogue and emotional weight
Treat the mech primarily as combat loadout and progression rather than a speaking mascot
Preserve light customization and future co-op compatibility
Keep sincere stakes and meaningful conflict
Allow colorful factions exaggerated weapons memorable bosses and rarity-readable loot
Avoid both relentlessly grey military bleakness and full parody
Use restrained humor only where it fits
Keep spectacle native to the setting
Provide direct access to missions armory upgrades shops challenges story and launch
Minimize between-run friction
Support PC and later Android navigation
Allow the interface to evolve visually with campaign progress
Do not require walking between stations
Author core layouts routes secrets objectives and major encounters
Allow bounded variation in selected formations reinforcements loot optional rooms modifiers or secondary conditions
Use fixed seeds or standardized conditions for comparable challenges
Do not make procedural generation the primary MVP level strategy
Keep the authored campaign as the main progression journey
Replay missions for records loot difficulty clears achievements challenges alternate objectives and build experimentation
Reward mastery rather than mandatory repetitive farming
Do not make the core structure a run-reset roguelite
Build one genuinely good replayable level first
Do not produce many missions in parallel before systems are proven
Create the next level only after shared systems and workflow are reusable
Treat two or three missions as a later internal milestone
Allow a fuller compact release to grow toward roughly six to ten substantial missions only after validation
Do not treat the first build as a public demo paid episode or Early Access launch
Defer packaging pricing and progress-transfer decisions
Validate fun buildability and production workflow first
Use one complete level as the immediate milestone
Add a second level after surrounding systems are in place
Support mission launch movement four simultaneous weapons a small enemy roster completion rewards or loot basic loadout or progression and immediate replay
Keep supporting systems minimal but real and connected
Avoid disconnected mechanic sandboxes and mock-only flow
Validate the full earn-adjust-replay loop
Keep rooms collision characters effects projectiles and simulation two-dimensional
Create depth through perspective shading shadows layering animation and optionally offline 3D-rendered sprites
Preserve predictable collision strong performance and later Android viability
Do not require runtime 3D
Show visible character and wall sides without using a strong isometric grid
Keep movement aiming navigation and collision mechanically planar
Prevent foreground walls props shadows and layering from hiding important threats or rewards
Preserve the chunky dimensional reference-game appearance

open_questions_added: Primary world conflict and first enemy faction identity
First level setting objective and climax
MVP weapon roster and loadout-content boundary
MVP enemy roster and boss boundary
Public demo release and monetization timing after internal validation

next_action.instruction: Ask exactly one concise A/B/C decision card selecting whether the game's main opposition is primarily a rogue-machine or automated industrial force, a biological or alien infestation, or a broader multi-faction science-fiction conflict whose one-level MVP focuses tightly on a single faction. Preserve the stylized action-science-fiction tone, mostly silent protagonist, menu-based hub, handcrafted missions with controlled variation, campaign-first replay, one-level internal vertical slice, fully 2D runtime, shallow angled top-down presentation, and the user's request to remain at the higher product and MVP level. Include enemy visual readability, weapon and loot fantasy, narrative flexibility, roster variety, asset reuse, boss potential, faction identity, future campaign expansion, and the danger of over-scoping the first level. Put the recommendation after all A/B/C options at the end of the card. Queue accepted decisions D-141 through D-150 and persist the complete batch at D-150 unless a context handoff or explicit user instruction requires earlier persistence.
